skill.name: testing-skills-with-subagents
skill.description: Guides creators through test-driven validation of AI skills using baseline failures, pressure scenarios, subagent tests, and refactor loops.
skill.summary: Test AI skills with RED-GREEN-REFACTOR scenarios before deployment.
skill.author: DYAI2025

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are non-executable Markdown examples and inline path references. No evidence found of malware, data exfiltration, real shell execution, prompt-injection override text, or hidden-file access beyond instructional examples. Publishable with a low-risk note because the skill teaches pressure testing and includes commands users may copy manually.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command detections are Markdown code fences or inline examples used to teach scenario writing and skill lookup. They are not executable source files and no automatic command execution path was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content appears inside Markdown examples and prose. No script, runtime hook, or executable file invokes those commands.

low_findings.title: Home Directory Skill Paths Are Instructional Examples
low_findings.description: The hidden-file and home-directory detections refer to example Claude skill library paths. They describe where a user might browse skills and do not read or modify files automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths are examples embedded in test scenarios and documentation variants. There is no evidence of file reads, writes, deletion, or credential harvesting.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Scheduler Detections Are Keyword Collisions
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm and at-command scheduling detections do not match real cryptographic or scheduling operations. They appear to be caused by ordinary prose, headings, dates, times, and YAML description text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted search found no md5, sha1, scheduler command, or cryptographic implementation. The flagged lines are documentation text.

low_findings.title: Pressure-Test Prompts May Be Misused Outside Testing
low_findings.description: The skill intentionally teaches realistic pressure scenarios that make agents choose under time, authority, and cost constraints. This is legitimate for evaluation, but users should keep scenarios bounded to test sessions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is explicit and central to the skill, but it is framed as testing rather than bypass or exfiltration. Risk depends on user context.

content.user_title: Test AI Skills With Subagent Scenarios
content.value_statement: Skill authors need proof that instructions work when agents face pressure and incentives to skip them. This skill provides a RED-GREEN-REFACTOR testing process for finding failures, fixing guidance, and retesting compliance.
actual_capabilities: Designs baseline tests that show how agents behave before a skill exists.
Creates pressure scenarios with time, authority, cost, and sunk-cost constraints.
Captures agent rationalizations so skill authors can address real failure modes.
Applies RED-GREEN-REFACTOR cycles to improve skill documentation.
Defines success criteria for determining whether a skill remains followed under pressure.
Provides a worked example for testing Claude skill discovery instructions.
limitations: It is guidance documentation and does not include an automated test runner.
Results depend on the model, prompt harness, and test conditions used by the author.
It is best for discipline-enforcing skills, not simple reference material.
Users must review generated scenarios to avoid applying pressure prompts in production work.

use_cases.title: Validate a New Skill Before Release
use_cases.description: Run baseline and pressure tests to prove the skill changes agent behavior before publishing it.

use_cases.title: Improve Team Instruction Documents
use_cases.description: Compare documentation variants and identify which wording makes agents follow required workflows.

use_cases.title: Audit Compliance Under Pressure
use_cases.description: Test whether agents keep following safety, testing, or review rules when tasks become urgent.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Baseline Test
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for the RED phase.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create three pressure scenarios to test a skill before the agent has access to it. Focus on realistic reasons the agent might skip the rule.

prompt_templates.title: Capture Rationalizations
prompt_templates.scenario: Document observed failures after baseline runs.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these agent responses and extract the exact rationalizations used to avoid the skill rule. Group repeated excuses into clear failure patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor the Skill Text
prompt_templates.prompt: Given these rationalizations, revise the skill guidance so it directly blocks each excuse while staying concise and actionable.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Full Test Campaign
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a RED-GREEN-REFACTOR test campaign for this discipline-enforcing skill. Include baseline runs, pressure scenarios, success criteria, and refactor checkpoints.

output_examples.input: A skill author wants to test whether agents always check a required testing skill before coding.
output: A set of baseline scenarios with deadline, confidence, and authority pressure.
A table of expected rationalizations to watch for during failed runs.
A retest plan that verifies the revised skill closes each loophole.

output_examples.input: An agent skipped a required workflow and claimed the case was too urgent.
output: A concise failure analysis showing the urgency rationalization.
Suggested skill wording that states urgency does not bypass the rule.
A follow-up pressure test that uses the same urgency trigger.

output_examples.input: A team is comparing several Claude Code instruction variants for skill discovery.
output: A comparison plan using the same scenarios across every variant.
Success criteria for checking whether agents discover, read, and follow skills.
A recommendation to keep the variant that survives pressure tests.

best_practices: Run a true baseline before writing or changing the skill.
Use realistic scenarios with at least three combined pressures.
Record exact agent rationalizations and address them directly in the skill.
anti_patterns: Writing a skill from assumptions without observing baseline failures.
Testing only easy prompts where the agent has no reason to skip the rule.
Stopping after one successful run instead of retesting after refactors.

faq.question: What does this skill help me test?
faq.answer: It helps test whether an AI skill changes agent behavior under realistic pressure.

faq.question: Does it run automated subagent tests?
faq.answer: No. It provides a process and test formats, not an executable harness.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What kinds of skills benefit most?
faq.answer: It works best for skills that enforce discipline, testing, review, or safety workflows.

faq.question: Why is the RED phase important?
faq.answer: The RED phase shows how agents fail without the skill, so fixes target real behavior.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: The audit found low risk. The flagged commands and paths are documentation examples, not executable code.
